entry.title: SAP HANA Database Server HDB Service Stopping Unexpectedly
entry.content:
Dear SAP Tech users,

For the past few weeks, our SAP HANA DB Server's HDB service has been stopping unexpectedly every other day. To resolve this, I manually log in and run the following command:

/usr/sap/NDB/HDB00/exe/sapcontrol -nr 00 -function RestartSystem HDB
Has anyone else encountered this issue? What could be causing the HDB service to stop, and are there any permanent solutions to prevent this from happening?

entry.title: Re: Using IPS to create a predictable password for non-SS...
entry.content:
Hi

The simplest way to achieve this is what is called "password migration"

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services/cloud-identity-services/configure-source-system-to-migrate-user-passwords-from-sap-successfactors-systems-to-identity-authentication

With this scenario your non-SSO will be able to login to SSFF with their old password (SSFF Password) and the passwords will be saved and managed in IAS after the first logon.

Create new passwords and share with the users anyway is not a good idea. As a second option i'd use an activation email if the password synch is not achievable in your scenario.

I'd recommend also to join the call "SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS/IPS): Open Office Hours for SAP SuccessFactors HCM". There's one session tomorrow. You'll be able to raise your questions there. Check the links at the top of the blog: https://community.sap.com/t5/product-and-customer-updates/migration-to-sap-cloud-identity-authentication-ias-ips-innovation-alert-amp/ba-p/271407

 

Hope it helps

Diego

entry.title: SAC Date Calculation in DA
entry.content:
I have a “payment method” dimension with an attribute for “Expected Settlement Time”. Based on the method chosen the attribute can be 1/2/3/4 days (the attribute is set as an integer).

I have a measure for incomes, a measure for outcomes and one created to copy the income in the expected settlement date, named “Expected Outgoing”.

My problem is that I don’t know how to write this calculation - copying the some 3-4 days after based on the attribute.

I’ve tried the below, but the syntax isn’t correct, as it mixes date and number of days:

DATA([d/Measures] = "Expected_Outgoing") = 
RESULTLOOKUP([d/Measures] = "Income", [d/Date] = DATEDIFF( [d/Date],[d/IL_D_POC_RPD_PMNTMTHD].[p/STLMNT_TIME],"DAY" ) )

DATA([d/Measures] = "Expected_Outgoing") =

RESULTLOOKUP([d/Measures] = "Income", [d/Date] = DATEDIFF( [d/Date], [d/IL_D_POC_RPD_PMNTMTHD].[p/STLMNT_TIME], "DAY" ) )

 

How should I approach that?

entry.title: Collective order in SAP - target and actual costs
entry.content:
Hi All,

 

I have a scenario, where I have order with couple of suborders. Confirmation is done by external MES system. Each of suborders are confirmed separately via MES. Control key in routings set with automatic GR. Coll. order with goods movement is activated in cost controlling in my production order type. While confirming  the suborder 101 movement is done and 261. 

The problem is between the end of the month and the beginning of the other one. Imagine the situation when all of suborders are confirmed in one month and the main order is confirmed in the next month. Then while having cost report in kkbc_ord (limited to the previous month) I cannot see the target's cost in suborders. There are 'artificial' variances, which will disappear after confirming the order in next month.

Is there any chance to have these target costs in suborders? 

Regards, thank you for your help!

entry.title: The ABAP Detective and the Errant Electrolyte
entry.content:
An aspect of sustainability is minimal resource use, in both the sense of lower costs, but also the avoidance of energy use that contributes to carbon dioxide emissions from fossil fuels. For an establishment on "all-electric" the latter is what might be called an externality. As a code and systems analysis detective, I look for areas of improvement.

Previous investigations into environmental monitoring tools are noted at the end; this case focuses on the efficiency and use of domestic hot water delivery. In the backwaters of the united states, standard appliances include a 30 or 40 gallon (sorry: X liters) tank with a heating source constantly attempting to maintain a set minimum temperature. Other areas use the "on-demand" approach, or hot-water-as-a-service, with a heating device close to the delivery point (let's not go into the 110 versus 220 volt infrastructure as a distraction).

To the crux of the biscuit: I set up a temperature monitor directly above the output of the hot water tank, connected to a Raspberry Pi that is polled by a central system. Other approaches would be locally collecting the data and uploading in scheduled bursts, pushing rather than pulling.

TMP102 gauge, copper, insulation. (Photo by Jim Spath)

(photo by Jim Spath)

This ad hoc wiring approach works in a non-intrusive way, as the sensor unit does not touch the copper pipes, nor contact running water. Typically throughout the day, hot water is not running, so the readings are close to ambient room temperature. When a tap is opened somewhere, preheated water goes near enough to tick up the temperature reading, then after the flows stops the temperature gradually degrades. Or, if the tank completely empties of heated water, the readings will correspondingly decline.

Standalone sensor:

TMP102 gauge with blips

(screenshot from Zabbix web client)

A few times during the day, temperature rises, then declines. Not a particularly useful data set if you want to ask questions like "how hot is the hot water" or "how long did the washer run?" or even "how much did this unit of work cost?". The human brain can glance at these shapes and discern patterns, like Sherlock Holmes checking out cigar-ash-flakes in a pursuit. Writing an algorithm is a bit trickier.

The Zabbix open source suite I'm using has tools to run calculations on data feeds; for instance in some cases I'd pull values in Celsius, then add a derived parameter converting to Fahrenheit. There are ways to "look back" to prior values to determine deltas (which I've not implemented yet). You can also set up triggers to perform actions such as alerting on worst case scenarios (like overheating or frozen pipes: Ow!).

I'll gloss over the gnarly details of the Sparkfun/QWIIC monitoring hardware/software and cut to the chase: only one specific gauge per Pi, without adding multiplexing I2C complexities, so I was faced with deploying 2 Pi's with the same gauge type, or add a different gauge on 1 Pi. I chose the latter. Then the case went into what we colloquially called a "mung" state. Not "hung" but not right either.

Pi and the OS

If I could, I'd "flame" here, showing the rapid pace of software development breaks things that worked a few short years ago in surprising ways. I'm using code that someone else wrote in bash shell scripting back in 2012 or so, and that logic doesn't need to be tweaked when a new Linux kernel or even a subsystem component is written. And generally, Python examples the vendors share keeps working from year to year. Upgrades to the running environment are non-breaking other than minor steps like "oh, use Python 3.12 now instead of 3.10" that are easy enough to resolve.

My first Pi platform had a "6.1" 32-bit Linux kernel version; more recently new installs have a "6.6" kernel, where 64-bit flavors are as stable as the 32-bit. Great, why would that matter? Somewhere in the matrix, Python grew an appendage, nicknamed "venv" meaning a virtual environment. Let's add containers, crates, and virtualization while we're at it. Except this is a $10 machine with a single purpose.

TMP102 and BME280

These 2 temperature sensors are available in the chosen "ecosystem" that connects the Pi brain to the digital sensors, which could also include humidity and barometric pressure. Since I had collected and deployed at least one of each, I assumed connecting both to 1 Pi would be simple. I had code samples in shell and Python for both. "Just" follow the manufacturers instructions and "pip" it into place (no, not the old DEC PIP peripheral to peripheral connector, the new python software package installer). Failure to launch:

 

$ pip install qwiic_bme280
error: externally-managed-environment

 

Oh, darn, this didn't work, and spit out a bunch of scary messages, especially this last dooms-day-scenario:

 

You can override this, at the risk of breaking \
 your Python installation or OS,  \
 by passing --break-system-packages. \
hint: See PEP 668 for the detailed specification.

 

"Break system packages" sounds ominous. Maybe don't take that approach. 

pip breakage

(screenshot from terminal session)

A more staid detective would go read the reference manuals, consult others online, re-read the source code, then diligently make necessary repairs. Others more reactive might literally check with their vendors and ask what's up with this. Investigators with a hacking tendency would start typing likely fixes and rout/route around the damage. Command line use of a venv turned out to be fairly simple:

 

python3 -m venv venv
venv/bin/pip3 install sparkfun-qwiic-bme280

[...]
Successfully installed \
 smbus2-0.5.0 \
 sparkfun-qwiic-bme280-0.9.0 \
 sparkfun-qwiic-i2c-1.0.0

 

No doubt more experienced Python developers are aghast at the hammer approach I used.

Getting readings at the command line would now work with 2 sensors; the new one shows:

 

$ venv/bin/python3.11  /path/to/sparkfun.py  temperature_celsius
16.2118

 

Fine, a 3-pipe problem rather than a trivial 2-pipe one. Except the whole intent is to automate sensor data collection, not have to run a command line manually. I'll leave out the messy trial-and-error details, other than to share that the Zabbix process debug steps boil down to seeing whether a parameter shows as "not supported" or returns an error message such as "got string, expecting a number" in the console, and checking the agent logs for clues (like: "The Qwiic BME280 device isn't connected to the system. Please check your connection" when I used the wrong sensor).

Stuffed inside an old shell script are new links to the monitor logic.

 

/path/venv/python3 /path/sparkfun-venv.py temperature_celsius

 

The middle section didn't need to have any virtual environment goo but for the sake of my future self looking at these sensors after the next incompatibility will possibly recall why.

Before going to the trouble of writing calculation or trigger steps in parameter definitions I used a handy UI feature in Zabbix that overlays 2 measurements in one chart. First, make sure both metrics are stable:

2 charts

(screenshot from Zabbix web client)

Then, add the second parameter to a dashboard widget. I didn't muck around with colors, lines, or transparencies, taking the defaults as good enough for the quick quality control / QC check.

2 temperature sensors on 1 chart

(screenshot from Zabbix web client)

Next problems to solve?

  • The 2 gauges differ under "no flow" states by over 1 degree.
  • Alerts/triggers
  • Validation/calibration

Calibration

Back in the last century, my polytechnic/polymath education included calibrating thermometers by determining how close their readings were to freezing boiling water temperatures. A memorable lesson in theory versus practice, tossing aside the novice assumption that a device always shows accurate values. How many degrees or tenths-of-degrees plus or minus the correct reading? (let's skip over side issues such as does water boil at exactly 100 degrees Celsius at any specific elevation or location).

What I'd probably investigate next is recording the temperature at the taps compared to the utility room infrastructure, particularly after hearing anecdotal comments such as "the kitchen sink faucet is not hot enough."

NOTES / REFERENCES

Shell:

  • https://znil.net/index.php/Zabbix:Template_Raspberry_Pi
  • https://znil.net/index.php?title=Temperatur_/_Spannung_etc._des_Raspberry_Pi_selbst_auslesen

Python:

  • http://rptl.io/venv
  • https://docs.python.org/3/library/venv.html
  • https://peps.python.org/pep-0668/
  • https://github.com/sparkfun/qwiic_bme280_py

SAP Community posts:

  • The ABAP Detective Takes The Summer Heat
  • The ABAP Detective Gets Their Clock Cleaned
  • The ABAP Detective Takes The Heat, or How To Cleanse Data

Also:

  • Intra home detectors

entry.title: One Network, Many Journeys
entry.content:
SAP Business Network began as a procurement platform – still its leading force today - then slowly started touching on additional operations. Today, the network includes supply chain, logistic and asset management transactions. As a result, the types of trading partners playing a role on the network flourished beyond the typical supplier-buyer relationship. Millions of trading partners interact daily in this constantly evolving environment, and not everyone had the same journey of joining SAP Business Network.

For organizations in need of a more holistic view of the network, we have put together a summarizing presentation exploring diverse journeys and multiple potential access points that suppliers and trading partners take to join and use SAP Business Network.

Click on the image to open the document

This deck is designed to provide a broad overview and valuable insights of how trading partners collaborate on SAP Business Network, highlighting key touchpoints and engagements with our teams.


We hope you’ll find this document helpful to map out your past, present and future journeys on SAP Business Network.

entry.title: Selling projects to leaders
entry.content:
Let’s start with one thing, if you struggle to sell the benefits of a good E-Commerce strategy to your Senior Leadership Team, you should quit your job, as the company won’t last long. However, there are some ways to enhance the role that E-Commerce can play in your business by getting the full backing of your superiors.

It’s no secret that Directors and Executives are interested in two major factors; Return on Investment and Client Satisfaction. E-Commerce professionals are very lucky that a well-developed strategy will cater for both of these in bucket loads. Automating your interactions with clients can cut the cost of transaction by up to two thirds, whilst also improving your clients’ experience of trading with you.

If I could offer one piece of advice, it would be to develop your skills in writing and presenting business cases. There is a lot to be said for taking the time to create a well prepared slide deck with all the relevant stats, expected ROI and time frames. Also, if the recent UK General Election has taught us anything, it’s that we need to fully cost our proposals before presenting them! Nothing is going to cut your presentation to a CEO or President short like not being able to directly answer their question. Do your research, prepare your slides, then present positively and confidently. Passion and exuberance are contagious and a vital part of your armory when requesting budget. Mentoring can also provide valuable input when preparing a business case. Is there someone you look up to within your company or a former boss that you maintain a great relationship with? If so, use them as a sounding board. Sometimes, there is no substitute for experience and great leaders love nothing more than having the opportunity to share that experience to help others.

Returning to my earlier point, Client Satisfaction goes hand in hand with ROI. The former can have a hugely positive impact on the latter if it’s done right. One phrase should be at the forefront of any good E-Commerce strategy; ‘How can I remove all obstacles to my clients’ ability to purchase?’ We only get two chances to impress prospective clients. Just two negative experiences will lead to you losing the client, probably forever. This is a small margin of error, so put yourself in your clients’ shoes from day one. Your customers need to be central to your thinking when designing and developing your E-Commerce offerings. Don’t forget that one of your best marketing tools is satisfied clients spreading word of your value. A successful leadership team will value and appreciate this methodology, making them far more likely to buy in to your way of thinking.

You also have one major advantage when approaching your bosses for a new E-Commerce project. Automating client experience reduces the cost and time of transaction, allowing your sales teams more time to be productive. E-Commerce teams typically run very lean and can deliver tangible benefits which are plain for all to see. Clients who integrate their ERPs with your business are also more patient if you make mistakes. A typical integration project can take up to three months (more for a large global client). This isn’t a cycle that businesses like to go through more often than they have to. Treat these clients well and you should be able to expand your sales portfolio with them, over a relationship lasting years.

Ultimately, you need to accept a leadership role yourself by driving E-Commerce within your business. Senior leaders rely on subject matter experts to steer their decision making. Guide them well, then deliver on your commitments!

Before approaching your leadership team with new ideas, keep the below in mind:

· Practice writing business cases

· Prepare and know your subject

· Be passionate

· Remove all the client’s obstacles

· Provide ROI

· Follow up and deliver

entry.title: Re: No Work Orders Downloaded
entry.content: Ensure that the OMDO filters are correctly assigned to the Persona and the user has the required authorization roles. Check if Mobile Data Object (MDO) sync rules are correctly configured for work orders. Also, validate the ODMO settings for notifications and verify if the Sync Criteria in MDK metadata allows downloading work orders and notifications.

entry.title: Re: Collective order in SAP - target and actual costs
entry.content:
In a collective order scenario, target costs are typically calculated at the header level, and suborders inherit costs upon final confirmation of the main order.
Since your main order is confirmed in the next month, the system does not distribute target costs to suborders in the previous month's KKBC_ORD report,
leading to temporary variances.

To see target costs in suborders, you can try:
1. Early settlement of suborders before month-end to reflect costs properly.
2. Using statistical cost reporting for suborders in KKBC_ORD.
3. Checking Cost Rollup settings in Costing Variant to enable cost distribution at suborder level.

entry.title: Re: Selling projects to leaders
entry.content:
A strong argument for strategic E-Commerce management! 💡 It’s undeniable that a company’s success today heavily depends on how effectively it leverages digital sales channels—not just for revenue growth but also for long-term customer retention.

One of the key points you highlight is the ability to clearly communicate ROI. Executives need solid numbers and compelling business cases to approve investments. Those who come prepared don’t just secure budgets—they position themselves as strategic thinkers.

I particularly appreciate your point about automation: Efficient digital processes reduce costs, enhance customer experience, and free up sales teams to focus on high-value tasks. Companies that fail to capitalize on this advantage risk falling behind the competition.

Ultimately, driving a successful E-Commerce strategy means taking ownership and seeing oneself as a leader—an often underestimated aspect. Those who present well-researched, practical solutions with confidence and passion will win leadership buy-in.

A great post with valuable, real-world insights!

entry.title: E-Learning for SAP LeanIX Application Ownership Model is available.
entry.content:
Hello SAP Signavio Community, 

SAP Business Transformation Management Value Map, along with the SAP LeanIX Academy team, is excited to announce we have released our E-Learning sessions for SAP LeanIX Application Ownership Model.  This E-Learnings is hosted on the SAP Enterprise Support Academy.

What the sessions is about:

Learn how to implement a proper application ownership model.  This micro-learning course enables you to better understand who the responsible parties are, how to identify them, and what options are available to get the relevant data into SAP LeanIX

 What are the session goals?

  • Understand what an application ownership model is
  • Understand the concept of application ownership
  • Understand the role and related tasks of an application owner
  • Understand the two different personas: application business owner and application technology owner (IT)
  • Learn how to identify owners and add them to the workspace

How to access?

NOTE:  You MUST be registered with the SAP Enterprise Support Academy in order to access the “direct links” to these courses below (and can do so right here if not currently registered).

Get started with SAP Enterprise Support Academy and Value Maps here. (Check out our step-by-step instructional video  on how to join SAP Enterprise Support Academy and Value maps.)

Direct link to the new E-Learning courses (once registered as stated above).

  • Course: SAP LeanIX:  Application Ownership Model

Do you need additional help?

Don't hesitate to get in touch with our team using the following form. (Please select the "Business Transformation Management" topic).

Regards, 

Leean De Loria

entry.title: Missing SAP predelivered content in Description of dangerous goods packages
entry.content: I'm trying to setup Product Compliance/Dangerous Goods but are stucked when updating Specify Description of Dangerous Goods Packages. entry.title: Msg No. M8534
entry.content:
HI

Balance not zero: 1.050.000,000-  debits: 1.050.000,000  credits: 0,000

Message No. M8534

Diagnosis

The system has discovered a difference between the debits and credits. This difference lies outside the tolerance limits set.

System Response

The system cannot post the document.

Procedure

If this message appears when you are processing a document online, you can change the amounts or enter new items until you can post the document.

You can change the Invoice Verification type, so that the document can be posted later in the background. If this message appeared when a document was verified in the background, you can now process the document.

entry.title: Embracing the Future: Chatbots, Copilots, and Agents in Procurement
entry.content:
One of my earliest memories of advanced technology involves voice recognition software. I was home from college, getting ready to fly back to school, when I overheard my dad on the phone. He was struggling to communicate with the airline’s automated system. He repeated my airline's name twice, carefully enunciated my flight number, and then, in frustration, shouted "Operator!" multiple times.

Since then, we’ve moved from automated systems through the phone to in-home virtual assistants like Siri, Google or Alexa. These advancements have made our lives significantly easier, and they continue to evolve.

Technology has grown by leaps and bounds – and it’s not just limited to voice recognition. It’s also playing a crucial role in simplifying complex procurement processes and enhancing user experiences. Today, voice recognition is just one of the many tools helping to automate and streamline operations.

Among the most transformative technologies are AI-assisted chatbots, copilots, and agents, which are revolutionizing how procurement professionals interact with systems and data.

Chatbots have been around for a while. They started off by handling basic queries, but their capabilities have expanded significantly with the advent of generative AI (GenAI). Now, chatbots can understand and process natural language, which makes each interaction more intuitive.

Copilots offer sophisticated, conversational capabilities. They act as strategic assistants and help procurement professionals navigate complex workflows and make informed decisions based on business context.

Agents are designed to autonomously perform complex, multistep workflows. They use AI to plan, reason, and execute tasks, allowing procurement teams to increase activity levels while also focusing on strategic objectives.

How You Can Use Chatbots and Copilots

In procurement, chatbots can guide users through procurement processes with predefined rules. They often provide standard responses to frequently asked questions on policies, processes, and procedures.

SAP's copilot, Joule, integrates seamlessly across SAP applications, including SAP Ariba and SAP Fieldglass solutions, to enhance procurement operations and leverage business context with historical data to guide users conversationally. Joule can also help with tasks like approvals, running reports, and answering questions like:

  • Which suppliers have responded to our sourcing event?
  • Where are the time sheets I need to approve? 
  • Who is the final approver for my high-profile project?

In addition to improving productivity, these technologies offer multiple benefits for procurement, such as:

  • Enhanced user experience. Intelligence is directly embedded into the user interface, ensuring interactions are part of everyday activities.
  • Streamlined processes. Automation of tedious and complex tasks like generating RFxs makes it easier to ensure compliance and maximize efficiency.
  • Better supplier management. Advanced insights help teams manage supplier performance data to reduce risks and strengthen critical relationships.

The Future of Agents

In many ways, the frustration my dad experienced with that early voice recognition system feels like a distant memory.  Today, we have numerous apps to help track flights. However, in some ways, the frustrations still feel relevant because some of these systems remain a work in progress. Regardless, it’s clear that technology is advancing with a focus on making it more intuitive and user-friendly.

Agents will revolutionize procurement by autonomously executing complex, multistep workflows and accelerating cross-functional operations, which will continuously run and ultimately free up resources. These agents will work alongside you and your colleagues, ensuring collaboration and oversight while enhancing efficiency and accuracy in procurement processes.

As these technologies evolve, they will transform procurement from a process-driven function to a strategic enabler.

Take the Next Step

Learn more about how these innovations are shaping the future of procurement. Join us for our March 27 webinar* with Pierre Mitchell, chief research officer at Spend Matters, to explore the potential ways you can use chatbots, copilots, and agents to drive procurement excellence in your organization.

Register today 

*This webinar will be available in multiple time zones.

entry.title: Re: Using IPS to create a predictable password for non-SS...
entry.content:
Hi,

Leaving the code in IPS will not cause an issue. This will be checked just once on the first login of the user and password then managed in IAS (if needed).


Technically you can keep that config forever in IPS and the new users in SSFF with loginmethod=PWD will be able to do the same. i.e use the password you set in SSFF initially. Once the user logins (and maybe change the pwd depending on the config you set up future local PWD changes have to be managed directly from IAS.


Clearly that's not the normal use case scenario and probably not intended by SAP to be used but if you already had a way to setup passwords for these new users in SSFF you can still do so and new users will be able to use initially the pwd you set in SSFF.


Also If these new users you are mentioned are in another IDP that supports SAML/OIDC you can add multiple IDPs to IAS and send the users to different IDPs based on rules

entry.title: Generate intercompany billing request based on the results of running allocation cycles (S4H Public)
entry.content:
We have a scenario where the parent company incurs shared costs (e.g., IT, HR, facility expenses) and allocates them to a subsidiary using intercompany cost allocation cycles (app Manage allocation cycles).

  • There is no time reporting, no activity types, and no end user reporting hours.
  • Only IC cost allocations are done via allocation cycles Manage Allocation cycles app  (e.g., from one cost center to another across company codes).
  • We need to generate an intercompany invoice (Intercompany billing request) based on these allocations.
  • We need to add also margin to such intercompany invoices
  • We need to reallocate also Revenues

We understand that there is only one solution to activate 4AN, 4AU, 16T scope items that relates to professional services industry to generate the intercompany billing request based on intercompany allocation cycles

  1. How can we add a margin to the intercompany invoice if we do not have activity types?

  2. To reallocate revenue cross-company is a very specific scenario. Is there a way to handle this within the standard Reassign Cost/Revenues process, or any other standard approach in SAP S/4HANA Cloud? Will the Intercompany Billing Request (ICBR) consider the amount of revenue reassigned between company codes?

  3. If SAP does not support this process in Public Cloud, what is the recommended approach to achieve this?

We appreciate any clear guidance based on actual system behavior.

Thank you!

entry.title: Re: Msg No. M8534
entry.content:
Sometimes you may need to delete unused items from the invoice to avoid a false error message. To do this, click on the "Delete Unselected Item" button at the bottom of the MIRO screen (it looks like the sync icon).

Of course, I assume that you have already entered the vendor/vat amounts in the header and selected some non-zero items.

entry.title: SAP IBP Configuration - Configure UOM Conversion in SAP IBP
entry.content:
In SAP IBP, Unit of Measure (UOM) conversion is the process of converting quantities from one unit of measure to another, ensuring consistency and accuracy in planning and reporting. This is particularly important when dealing with products that are measured in different units across various stages of the supply chain.

To set up UOM conversion in SAP IBP, you typically need to:

  1. Create and add the UOM Master Data Type (MDT): This involves defining the units of measure and their conversion factors.
  2. Define UOM conversion factors: These are attributes or key figures that specify how one unit converts to another (e.g., 1 pallet = 1023 cartons).
  3. Configure planning levels: Ensure that the planning levels incorporate the UOM conversion factors.
  4. Enable UOM conversion in key figures: This allows the system to automatically convert quantities during planning and reporting.

This setup helps in maintaining accurate data and facilitates better decision-making by providing a consistent view of quantities across different units.

In this article, I wll set up the configuration for Unit of Measure conversion for the S&OP process.

Create Attributes

Create the following attributes used for the unit of measure conversion. Use the values in the table:

IDNameTypeLength
I49UOMCONVFACTORUOM Conversion FactorDECIMAL18, 6
I49UOMDESCRUOM DescriptionNVARCHAR40
I49UOMIDUOM FromNVARCHAR20
I49UOMTODESCRUOM To DescriptionNVARCHAR40
I49UOMTOIDUOM ToNVARCHAR20

Reference SAP IBP Configuration - Create an Attribute in SAP IBP

Create the Master Data Types and Planning Levels

1. Create the simple master data type I49UOMTO using the following details.

FieldValue
NameUOM To
DescriptionUOM To
Master Data TypeSimple
ATTRIBUTES: 
I49UOMTOIDKey
I49UOMTODESCR 

2. Change the master data type I49PRODUCT by adding the I49UOMID and I49UOMDESCR attributes.

Because adding a new attribute to a master data type that have uploaded data for, you must upload data again for the changed master data type.

3. Create the compound master data type I49UOMCONVERSION, made up of simple master data types I49PRODUCT and I49UOMTO, using the following details:

FieldValueSimple Master Data Types
NameUOM Conversion 
DescriptionUOM Conversion 
Master Data TypeCompound 
Add Simple Master Data Types:  
  I49PRODUCT
  I49UOMTO
ATTRIBUTES:  
PRDIDKey 
I49UOMTOIDKey 
I49UOMCONVFACTOR  

4. Assign the simple master data type you just created and the attributes of I49PRODUCT to the planning area:

  • I49PRODUCT
  • I49UOMTO

Add attributes of new simple master data type I49UOMTO to planning area

Add new attributes I49UOMID and I49UOMDESCR in simple master data type I49PRODUCT to planning area

5. Create the PRODUOMTO planning level, which will be the base planning level for the UOM Conversion.

FieldValueRootConversion SourceConversion Target
IDPRODUOMTO   
DescriptionProduct / UOM To   
Master Data Type    
I49PRODUCT:    
 PRDIDXX 
 PRDDESCR   
 PRDFAMILY   
 ABCCODE   
 I49UOMID   
 I49UOMDESCR   
I49UOMTO:    
 I49UOMTODESCR   
 I49UOMTOIDX X

a. Select New, choose the lowest time profile level as None, and choose OK.

There are no time attributes assigned to this planning level.

b. Assign attributes

6. Create the PERPRODCUSTUOMTO planning level, which will be the base planning level for the UOM Conversion Calculation.

FieldValueRoot
IDPERPRODCUSTUOMTO 
DescriptionPeriod / Product / Customer / UOM To 
Time Attributes  
 PERIODID (add via Change Assignment) 
 MonthlyX
 Quarterly 
 Year 
Attributes:  
I49UOMTO  
 I49UOMTODESCR 
 I49UOMTOIDX
I49CUSTOMER  
 CUSTDESCR 
 CUSTIDX
 CUSTREGION 
I49PRODUCT  
 PRDIDX
 PRDFAMILY 
 PRDDESCR 
 I49UOM 
 I49UOMDESCR 
 ABCCODE 

 

Create the UOMCONVERSION Key Figure as an Attribute as Key Figure

1. Set I49UOMCONVFACTOR as Attribute as key figure and assign the PRODUOMTO planning level.

2. Make the following settings for the I49UOMCONVFACTOR key figure.

  • Edit Allowed: Editable in the Current or Future
  • Aggregation Mode: AVG
  • Disaggregation Mode: Copy Value
  • Stored and Calculated: Stored

3. Check that the Request level calculation for I49UOMCONVFACTOR exists and that it is the average of the PRODUOMTO level.

I49UOMCONVFACTOR@REQUEST = AVG(“I49UOMCONVFACTOR@PRODUOMTO”)

UOMCONVFACTOR is selected as an input and is selected as Stored.

4. Create a new key figure called TARGETQTY at the PERPRODCUST planning level with the following data.

FieldValue
Key Figure IDTARGETQTY
NameTarget Quantity
DescriptionTarget Quantity
Base Planning LevelPERPRODCUST
StoredX
Edit AllowedEditable in current or future
Aggregation ModeSum
Disaggregation ModeEqual Distribution
ProportionalitySame Key Figure - Stored Values
Convert UsingI49UOMCONVFACTOR

 

5. Add the calculation so the Target Quantity is calculated by the stored value times the UOM Conversion.

TARGETQTY@PERPRODCUSTUOMTO = “TARGETQTY@PERPRODCUST” * “I49UOMCONVFACTOR@PRODUOMTO”

Select Inputs and in the dialog box that displays, ensure that I49UOMCONVFACTOR and TARGETQTY are selected as an input and selected as Stored.

6. Change the Request level calculation for TARGETQTY so that it is the sum of the PERPRODCUSTUOMTO level. TARGETQTY@REQUEST = SUM(“TARGETQTY@PERPRODCUSTUOMTO”)

In the Input Key Figures dialog box that displays, ensure that TARGETQTY is selected as an input, but not selected as Stored.

7. Activate planning area and select the Full Scope with Dependencies to include the Related Master Data Types.

Load Data into the New Master Data Types

1. Load data into your new I49UOMTO and I49UOMCONVERSION master data types.

Reload data into the I49PRODUCT master data type, because you added the I49UOM and I49UOMDESCR attributes.

2. Load data into your Target Qty key figure.

 

Test the Solution in the Planner Workspace

1. Test your solution by creating a Workspace using the information from the table. After you view the data, choose Settings and change the Target UOM to view the data in another UOM.

Use the following values for Time:

FieldValue
Time PeriodMonthly
FromCurrent Month
ToCurrent Month +12
RollingYes

Use the following values for Attributes:

FieldValue
Product IDSelected
Customer IDSelected
I49UOMTODESCRSelected

Use the following values for Key Figures:

FieldValue
Target QTYSelected
UOM ToEA

Change UOM to Boxes, the values of Target Qty are all changed. 

Target Qty = Target Qty@UOM EA * I49UOMCONVFACTOR = 12,155,000 * 0.083333 = 1,012,912.615

entry.title: Re: authorization required access to login sap btp trial on eclipse
entry.content:
Hi Acetedi,

I encountered a similar issue recently and was able to resolve it through trial and error. Here’s what worked for me:

  1. Ensure that you are using the latest version of Eclipse. If not, update it to the most recent version.
  2. Once Eclipse is up to date, delete your existing ABAP trial instance in SAP BTP.
  3. Create a new ABAP trial instance and log in again using Eclipse ABAP.

This approach helped me resolve the issue, and I hope it works for you as well. 

Happy Learning!

Preview
 
Regards,
Vineeth.

entry.title: Dynamic Interface on Cloud Integration - part 1
entry.content:
Last year at SAP TechEd, I was fortunate to showcase some of our self-developed innovations at Ferring (in case you missed it, you can see the recording here: https://www.sap.com/events/teched/virtual/flow/sap/te24/catalog/page/catalog/session/1723578590675001AnGg). One of them was an implementation of a dynamic interface.

This weekend I finally had some time to at least start sharing our approach with the community. 

I’ll break this article into:

  • Problem/Context (this link)
  • Our approach
  • Git into Partner Directory
  • Generic iflow
  • Automated unit testing
  • Limitations and Future plans

Problem/Context

Nowadays we have many requests for new interfaces (mostly xml based) where some simple transformation needs to be applied and then sent to a target (via SFTP, HTTP, AS2, etc). I think this is a very common request that can become a pattern. Together with the team we discussed that we will be able to become more agile when receiving such simple requests, and not need to assign a developer to create new interfaces for every new scenario that would fall into this pattern.

Example

Let's say you have xml below coming from S4 and you want to apply different transformations to it depending on it's content and also send to different target urls according to some content rules.

Example one for APAC region:

 

 

 

 

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<root>
    <process>CreateBatch</process>
    <region>APAC</region>
    <prod_date>20240730</prod_date> <!-- format of dates in S4 is yyyymmdd -->
    <quantity>1000</quantity>
    <batch>1</batch>
    <batchplant>CH01</batchplant>
    <country>SG</country>
    <material>12345678901</material>
    <!-- other fields not relevant for this demo -->
</root>

 

 

 

 

Example 2 for EMEA region

 

 

 

 

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<root>
	<process>CreateBatch</process>
	<region>EMEA</region>
	<prod_date>20240730</prod_date> <!-- format of dates in S4 is yyyymmdd -->
	<quantity>1000</quantity>
	<batch>12345</batch>
	<batchplant>CH01</batchplant>
	<material>12345678901</material>
	<country>CH</country>
	<!-- other fields not relevant for this demo -->
</root>

 

 

 

 

Let's assume that we need to have the following rules / requirements

Ok, we've got our requirements, how would you implement them?

If I think in cloud integration terms, perhaps creating an iflow that would extract the required fields and have all these routers with different XSLT transformations for each router to finally send it to the different urls. This would lead to an iflow specific to this particular scenario, which in the end after several requests like this becomes hard to maintain all these independent iflows.

In this part we've explored the motivation for developing such a generic iflow. In the next part we'll have a look at the architecture followed and we would detail the implementation for this specific demo scenario.

In the meantime you can think and comment of your own approach to this problem

entry.title: Automating Message Retries in SAP CPI with the Data Store Channel
entry.content:
Introduction

Handling failed messages efficiently is a critical aspect of SAP Cloud Integration (SAP CPI). While CPI does not offer a built-in feature to automatically resend failed messages, several SAP community-driven solutions exist using JMS queues and data stores. This blog introduces an alternative approach that leverages the Data Store Channel to effectively manage and retry failed messages.

Scenario Overview

In this scenario, we have two iFlows:

  1. Main iFlow – This is any custom iFlow developed in your tenant where you want to implement the retriggering mechanism.(iflow name:Test_Mainiflow)

 

  1. Retrigger iFlow – This iFlow utilizes the Data Store Sender Channel to pick up failed messages and resend them to the original iFlow.(iflow name:Test_ReprocessFailedMessageDataStore)

 

The Main iFlow consists of two integration processes:

  1. Initial Integration Process: Receives the message payload via a sender channel, which can be any adapter or a timer function depending on the requirement.

  2. Retrigger Integration Process: Retriggers failed messages stored in the data store.

Both integration processes utilize a Content Modifier to set the standard SAP sender header, which is later used for retriggering purposes. Additionally, a Process Call directs messages to the main local process where the core integration logic is maintained

The local process contains the core logic, including filtering, mapping, and payload manipulation. For simplicity, the following two steps are included:

  • Content Modifier: Sets integration-related parameters.
  • Process Call: Forwards the processed data to the receiver.

Additionally, an Exception Subprocess is incorporated to capture error details in case of failures.

Below are the key parameters used in the content modifier in local integration process:

 

ParametersPurpose
DatastoreNameStores failed messages.
EmaildEmail recipients for failure notifications.
interface_idUnique integration identifier.
SAP_ApplicationIDStores unique identifiers such as PO or IDOC numbers.
end_pointProcess direct address to identify each interface uniquely. Make sure each interface should have unique address value.
source_payloadOriginal payload received from the sender.
email_subjectSubject for error alert emails.
SAP_ReceiverDisplays receiver system name in the monitoring page.
SAP_SenderDisplays sender system name in the monitoring page.

Content modifier parameter in exception subprocess

Exception Subprocess Content Modifier Header

 

Parameter

Purpose

cpi_error

Captures error details (${property.CamelExceptionCaught}).

error_type

Differentiates error types; retry logic applies only to connectivity errors. Retriggering failed message should only happen in case connectivity issue and not for all errors like mapping or any syntax or any other configuration errors which won't be resolved even if message is reprocessed.

 

Exception Subprocess Content Modifier Property

A Process Call within the Exception Subprocess routes messages to the local process, ensuring that retry logic is applied only in the case of retriable errors (e.g., connectivity issues, not mapping or syntax errors).

Below is the local integration process to send data to receiver system.

Here we are using HTTP receiver channel where config is such that message will fail in CPI to test our retry logic.

Content modifier in the exception subprocess

Header cpi_error will store the error details but property error_type will have constant value connection(can be any constant value)

 

Below is the local process which is getting called from exception subprocess.

The retrigger mechanism is implemented using a router with the following conditions:

Condition maintained for the Data store branch 

${property.error_type} = 'connection' and ${property.Temp_Sender} != 'CPI'

This condition ensures that messages are only stored in the data store if they originally failed due to a connectivity issue and were triggered from the sender system. If a retriggered message initiated by the Data Store Channel iFlow(Sender as 'CPI') fails again, it will not be stored again to prevent an infinite loop.

Messages are stored in the Data Store along with headers, using the message ID as the unique entry ID. It is crucial to enable the "Include Message Headers" checkbox to ensure retriggering functions correctly.

 

Retriggering Failed Messages

A second iFlow is designed to retrieve failed messages from the data store and resend them to the original iFlow. The retry mechanism follows these steps:

  1. The iFlow picks up messages from the data store.

  2. It attempts to resend the message up to three times.

  3. If the message is successful in any of the retry attempts, it is deleted from the data store.

  4. If the message remains unsuccessful, it is deleted from the data store and an error alert email is sent.

Below is the main integration process which picks up the message from data store and sends it bak to main iflow.

 

Data store sender channel

The parameters used in the Data Store Sender Adapter are quite similar to those in the JMS Sender Adapter. While most of them are self-explanatory, you can refer to the official SAP documentation for a detailed explanation of each parameter:

Data Store Sender Adapter Documentation

The receiver channel is configured as a Process Direct Adapter, with the address stored in the header variable end_point. This header must be defined in the main iFlow and should be unique for each iFlow in your tenant. If the configured address is incorrect, the reprocessing mechanism will not function as expected.

If retriggered messages fail again, Exception Subprocess handles the failure.

Final Reprocessing Mechanism

A router checks the SAP standard header SAP_DataStoreRetries, ensuring the retry limit is not exceeded before marking the message as permanently failed.

Once a message is retriggered from the data store back to the main iFlow, it is processed again. If the main/original iFlow successfully processes the message, it is immediately removed from the data store. However, if the message fails again, the exception subprocess in the retrigger iFlow is triggered, as illustrated in the image below.

 

At this stage, a router evaluates the standard SAP header 'SAP_DataStoreRetries' to determine the retry count. If the message has not yet exceeded the configured retry limit, it proceeds to the retry branch with error end, keeping it in a 'retry' status. This means the Data Store Sender Channel will attempt to resend the message after the predefined interval. However, if the retry limit is reached, the message follows the error branch and is not retried further.

Also since main iflow failed with the error router in the main/original iflow will make sure message is not stored in the data store. 

Below is how actual reprocessing mechanism will work.

Triggered test payload from POSTMAN

Initial Failure: Message failed in CPI and datastore entry is created. You can see the values of Sender, receiver and application message id as well.

 

 

 

First Retry: The message is retried but is not re-stored in the data store as it is coming from integration paramter 'sender' as 'CPI' and 'error_type' header is 'connection'

 

Second Retry:

Third/Final Retry:

 

 

Message entry from data store is deleted.

Key Considerations:

  • This retrigger mechanism is only applicable for asynchronous integrations.

  • The retry logic is specifically implemented for connectivity errors. If required, it can be extended to all errors by modifying the exception subprocess in main iflow logic by removing the content modifier.

  • For better scalability, consider implementing a centralized error handling iFlow that processes error details and forwards them to data store retrigger iflow.

  • Ensure that runtime configuration is marked as * to retain all necessary headers during retriggering.

  • The first retry happens very quickly as it depends on the polling interval timer. If a message arrives close to the end of the interval (e.g., at the 9:50-second mark of a 10-minute interval), it will be picked up in the next poll almost immediately, so choose your poll interval accordingly.

  • Currently, the retrigger mechanism sends the message back to the source, requiring it to go through all processing steps again before reaching the receiver. This may not be ideal if the iFlow involves multiple transformations, mappings, or complex processing logic.

    One possible workaround to optimize this process is to break the iFlow into multiple local integration processes. Instead of retriggering the entire iFlow, the retrigger mechanism could directly call the relevant subprocess where the failure occurred. However, implementing this approach would require additional adjustments, such as storing the payload after key processing steps and maintaining a flag to determine the last successful subprocess execution.

 

 

Conclusion

This blog demonstrates one approach to reprocessing failed messages using the Data Store Channel in SAP CPI. While not the only method available, this approach ensures efficient message retrying and prevents unnecessary message duplication.

If you have any questions or feedback, feel free to share them in the comments section.

References:

  • Message Retry Using Process Direct and Data Store

  • Configure Asynchronous Messaging with Retry Using JMS

  • Retry Failed Messages in CPI

Happy Integrating! 🚀

entry.title: Enhancement - Risk analysis to consider or dismiss empty(Dummy) values in authorizations
entry.content:
The Purpose of this Blog is to explain the enhancement for considering or dismissing(exclude) Empty (Dummy) authorization values in GRC Risk Analysis.

We observe that the risk analysis engine does not consider the empty (dummy) authorization values maintained in the authorization objects (like object S_USER_GRP field CLASS with value ' ') starting from GRC AC 12 SP16. The most probable symptom is the disappearance of some permission level violations.

Before AC 12.0 SP16, the risk analysis considered the empty authorization values (dummy values), but with the introduction of SP16, they automatically became excluded. SAP has come up with the enhancement after we reporting this issue. With this enhancement you can decide how the risk analysis should handle the empty(Dummy) authorization values. You can either restore the pre-SP16 behavior or go with the current one by excluding Empty authorizations.

Solution is : Please implement the attached Correction Instruction in the note 3482508 or upgrade to the equivalent Support Package. Please be informed that you also need to perform the Manual Activities (if the objects are not created by Snote) after the Note implementation.

A new SPRO parameter has been introduced: 1056 Consideration type for empty authorization values

Path: SPRO--> Governance, Risk and Compliance --> Access Control-->Maintain Configuration Settings

This parameter decides whether the risk analysis considers or skips the empty values in the authorizations.

Possible values for the SPRO 1056 parameter:


If set to '1' -> empty auth values will NOT be considered (risk analysis behaves as of SP16)
If set to '2' -> empty auth values will be considered (risk analysis behaves as worked before SP16)

entry.title: From Errors to Efficiency: AI-Driven Script Repair in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

Modern businesses rely heavily on SAP Integration Suite to orchestrate data and processes between various on-premise and cloud systems. In such a complex environment, custom scripts—particularly Groovy scripts and XSLTs—play a critical role in custom data transformation and business logic. However, sometimes during the testing phase but also in productive scenario, these scripts can crash due to human error or master data quality, requiring manual debugging and re-deployment.

This article introduces an approach for using Generative AI to automate script repair within SAP Integration Suite. By combining automated monitoring, email-based interaction, and an AI-driven code remediation service, development teams can detect errors quickly, generate fixes, and validate changes without extensive manual intervention. The goal is to minimize downtime, streamline testing, and accelerate the resolution of script-related issues.

 

Scenario Overview

Imagine a scenario where an iFlow in SAP Integration Suite suddenly encounters a Groovy script error during message processing. The tool will capture the error details, notify the appropriate team or user, and then (optionally) invoke an AI-powered code assistant to suggest a fix. Once a fix is generated, it can be tested in a controlled environment. If successful, the updated script is automatically deployed to the main integration flow, and a final notification is sent to confirm resolution.

In the diagram, the error is first caught by the Monitoring Agent, which relays the details to the Mail Agent. The Mail Agent, in turn, sends a structured message to the user and awaits instructions. Depending on user feedback (e.g., “Yes, attempt a fix”), the updated script is passed to the Artifact Agent for versioning and redeployment in partnership with Content Agent .The Testing Agent replays the initial inbound message against the newly deployed script to verify that the fix is successful. Finally, the user receives a confirmation email containing the results.

 

Architecture Overview

 

 Picture 1 - Architecture Overview

 

Technical Setup

SAP Integration Suite Configuration

Each iFlow artifact is configured to save each failed process logs and parameters to a database, in our case the internal SAP Integration Suite Datastore. It will save the MPL ID, body, headers and properties that were used before the groovy script error was caught. This way, it will be able to test with the actual data caught before the error, so the developer has full confidence that the script was resolved correctly.


Monitoring & Notification

The Monitoring Agent listens for errors (e.g., via Datastore polling or possibly via webhook subscription).
Once an error is detected, the agent captures the message payload, relevant headers, and the failing script reference and sends the relevant data to the developer via Email (in our case).


Email/Communication Channel

The Mail Agent could trigger an email or ticketing system message containing error details.
The user can reply with commands (e.g., “Fix,” “Test,” or “Ignore”) that the Mail Agent interprets to decide the next action.


Generative AI Integration

The Content Agent is a service that takes the failing script and error context as input.
It uses a pre-trained Assistant model to suggest or generate an updated script that addresses the specific error message. The Assistant has clear instructions about what it's about to receive, how to structure the data, what tools to use ( for example code_interpreter ). Security and data privacy are maintained by sending only necessary information to the AI service.

 

Picture 2 - Creating an assistant


Testing & Deployment

The Artifact Agent handles version control and deployment to a safe environment.
The Testing Agent replays the stored payload to confirm that the updated script runs without errors.
If successful, the final version is automatically promoted to the main environment, and a summary is sent to the user.

 

Agents

 

 Picture 3 - Agents

 

 

Monitoring Agent

  • Purpose: Continuously listens for error events in SAP Integration Suite logs.
  • Key Actions:
    1. Captures error details and payload.
    2. Passes error information to the Mail Agent

 

Mail Agent

  • Purpose: Acts as the primary communication between the tool and the user (or team).
  • Key Actions:
    1. Sends error notifications.
    2. Receives user instructions (e.g., “Fix,” “Test,” “Cancel”).
    3. Routes commands to the appropriate next agent.

 

Content Agent

  • Purpose: Automates the script remediation process using Generative AI.
  • Key Actions:
    1. Receives the failing script, error stack trace, and relevant payload context.
    2. Generates a corrected version of the script.
    3. Returns the updated script to the Mail Agent or Artifact Agent.

 

Artifact Agent

  • Purpose: Manages script versioning and deployment to the SAP Integration Suite.
  • Key Actions:
    1. Creates a new version of the Groovy script.
    2. Deploys the updated artifact to a safe environment for testing.
    3. If testing is successful, promotes the artifact to the main environment.

 

Testing Agent

  • Purpose: Validates the updated script in a safe environment.
  • Key Actions:
    1. Retrieves the stored payload and test parameters.
    2. Invokes the iFlow with the updated script.
    3. Collects and reports results back to the Mail Agent.

 

Sample scenario

1. An error is detected, and an email is sent to the developer.

 

 

 Picture 4 - Email received by developer

2. Developer replies to the email with try_repair=1 that triggers the Artifact and Content Agents to download the current groovyscript, and together with Generative AI, they will try to find a fix for the script.

3. The developer receives a new email, this time saying that the agents found a fix for the script and waits further instructions. If desired, developer can reply with try_test=0 so the Testing Agent is not called to duty and the Artifact Agent directly creates a new version and deploys  or it can reply with try_test=1 so the normal workflow is triggered.

Picture 5 - Fix found

Deploying without testing is not what we really want, but the tool does what the developer wants.

Picture 6 - try_test=0 was sent

 

4. Developer replies to the email with try_test=1 that triggers the Artifact and Testing Agents to extract the new version provided by the Content Agent together with the failed run parameters and test in a dummy flow the new version of the script. If testing is succesful and no error occurs, the Testing Agent will notify the Email Agent who will send the results to the developer.

 

Picture 6 - Testing is succesful

If the testing fails for the first script iteration, the Content, Artifact and Testing Agents will talk to each other for a fixed amount of times. If even after the last attempt, the script fails, the Testing Agent will notify the developer that the task is above it's pay grade and needs help.

If the testing is done and succesful, the developer can reply with try_deploy=1 and the agents will create a new version and deploy the updated groovyscript.

Picture 7 - Deployment succesful

 

Future improvements

The tool can be easily adapted to handle other types of errors, like XSLTs for example, or even user-defined functions in mappings. Also, the communication with the developer can be adapted to various channels, like ticketing systems, Teams/Slack messages, notifications through SAP Process Automation, event-based triggers, etc.

It can be adapted to store via CI/CD pipelines the old/new script versions, so nothing is lost. It can be paired with a database so all the run parameters are easily stored and called whenever they're needed, like for example comparing older versions performance or using them to fix a current issue.

 

Conclusions

This architecture demonstrates how Generative AI can be seamlessly integrated into SAP Integration Suite to automate script repairs. By breaking down the workflow into specialized agents—each responsible for monitoring, communication, AI-driven fixes, testing, and deployment—you can drastically reduce the mean time to resolution for groovy scripts or even XSLT errors. Furthermore, this modular approach allows each agent to evolve independently, providing flexibility for future enhancements (e.g., more advanced testing strategies, integration with additional AI services, or improved notification mechanisms).

Ultimately, the solution ensures that developers and functional teams can focus on higher-value tasks rather than manual debugging and redeployment, leading to improved productivity, faster turnaround, and a more resilient integration landscape.

For any questions or suggestions, feel free to post here or in the special Q&A Section.

Thank you!

entry.title: Ensuring Data Consistency in the Extraction of SuccessFactors Data to SAP Datasphere
entry.content:
When transferring data from SuccessFactors to SAP Datasphere via the OData API, key challenges often arise around ensuring data completeness, accuracy, and integrity.

  1. Loss or omission of records during the transfer.
  2. Picklist attributes access.

 In this blog, we will dive into these challenges and offer a technical solution to ensure the data is accurately retrieved and mapped in the SAP Datasphere.

 

Ensuring Complete Data Transfers from SAP SuccessFactors to Datasphere via OData API

While extracting data from SF via OData API, users may encounter missing records in their target table. This issue typically arises due to pagination limitations in OData API or inconsistencies in the data extraction process due to large data volumes. Complete data transfer is essential for accurate reporting and analysis.

Scenario

  • Source: SuccessFactors OData API.
  • Destination: SAP Datasphere table.
  • Process: Data extraction via dataflows in SAP Datasphere.

Symptoms

  • Some records are not transferred to the target table.
  • Dataflow execution completes without errors.
  • Discrepancies are identified during data validation.

Root Cause

The OData API in SuccessFactors handles large datasets using pagination. Without proper configuration, the following issues can occur:

  • Dynamic Data Changes: Data in SuccessFactors can change during the extraction process (e.g., records updated or deleted).
  • Pagination Gaps: If the API paginates data dynamically, it may skip records or retrieve incomplete pages due to changes in the underlying dataset during runtime.

Solution

Setting the OData Parameter: paging=snapshot.
The paging=snapshot parameter ensures that the OData API captures a consistent snapshot of the dataset at the time of the query execution. This prevents records from being missed during the extraction process.

Step-by-step guide to implement

Step 1: Access the Dataflow

  • Log in to SAP Datasphere.
  • Navigate to the Data Builder and open the Dataflow where the issue occurs.

Step 2: Modify the OData Query Parameters

  • In the Source Node of the dataflow, locate the OData Parameter for the SuccessFactors data source.

  • Click on the Edit OData Parameters to open the OData Parameters dialogue and select the option “paging” and enter the value “snapshot”.     

Step 3: Save and Deploy the Dataflow

  • Save the changes to the dataflow.
  • Delete the data from the Target Table and Re-Run the dataflow and monitor the logs to confirm successful data extraction.
  • Compare the source data in SuccessFactors with the data in the SAP Datasphere table to ensure no records are missing.

 

Integrating Picklist Attributes of SAP SuccessFactors in Datasphere

 When integrating SAP SuccessFactors with SAP Datasphere via the OData API, a common issue users face is that picklist attributes do not appear in the target table after creating a Data Flow. This can lead to incomplete or unreadable data in reports and dashboards, as fields like Job Title, Department, Business Unit, and Country might only display IDs instead of meaningful descriptions.

Root Cause

Picklist fields in SF are exposed as navigation attributes by SF OData API, which by default are not consumed by SF OData connection.

Workaround

  • For source tables from OData remote connections, you can edit the depth properties of a selected source table in the OData Properties section.

      

  • Click Edit OData Properties to open the OData Properties dialogue. You can set the depth of the source table to either 1 or 2.

The depth of an OData object refers to the level of related entities that are included in the response when querying the OData service. The depth is by default set to 1 so that only the properties of the requested entity are returned. You can change the depth to 2 to include a second level. Depth is useful when you want to optimize performance by controlling the amount of data returned in a single request.

Limitations

When the depth is set to 2, the Columns section shows the columns from the two levels and the following problems can occur:

  • The Data Preview cannot show more than one collection. The columns from the second collection cannot be previewed.
  • The Data Flow run will fail if the dataset is large. Retaining columns from just one level and deleting the rest fixes the issue. To improve performance, store picklist values separately and join them in Dataflows or as Dimension Associations.

Documentation links relevant to integrating SAP SuccessFactors with SAP Datasphere:

SAP Help Portal

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2735876

https://me.sap.com/notes/0003136568

Acquiring, Preparing, and Modeling Data with SAP Datasphere | Help Guide

entry.title: Merge Different XML Structures by key with Groovy Code in SAP CPI
entry.content:
In SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI), efficiently merging XML documents is crucial for integrating diverse systems. This blog post explores how to enhance your integration scenarios by employing Groovy scripts for XML merging in SAP CPI. XML merging becomes essential when you need to pull and merge data from multiple sources.

In this scenario we have two xml from different sources. Where we couldn’t use enrich in the flow to merge them. We are going to merge them according to their emails.

Prerequisites

Before putting the code in flow, you need to have these properties:

Name of property           Description                                                             In our scenario

firstBodyData                    first xml body

secBodyData                     second xml body

firstArea                             fields to be matched on in first xml                         root/value/mail

secArea                              fields to be matched on in second xml                   root/Resources/emails/value

firstStruct                           structure to be matched from first xml                     root/value

secStruct                            structure to be matched from second xml               root/Resources

Another thing to be aware of is this code can only work when data are under one level in the structure. For example:

 

<root>
	<value>
		...
	</value>
	<value>
		...
	</value>
	<value>
		...
	</value>
	...
</root>

 

The Groovy Script

 

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message;
import java.util.HashMap;
import groovy.xml.*
import groovy.xml.MarkupBuilder ;

def getNodeByPath(node, path) {
    def pathElements = path.split('/')
    def current = node

    pathElements.each { element ->
        current = current."$element"
    }
    return current
}
    
def Message processData(Message message) {

    properties = message.getProperties();
    firstBodyData = properties.get("firstBodyData") as String
    secBodyData = properties.get("secBodyData") as String
    firstArea = properties.get("firstArea");
    secArea = properties.get("secArea");
    firstStruct = properties.get("firstStruct");
    secStruct = properties.get("secStruct");

    def firstBody = new XmlParser().parseText(firstBodyData)
    def secBody = new XmlParser().parseText(secBodyData)

    firstArea1 = firstArea.split('^[A-z0-9]*/')
    firstAfterRoot = firstArea1[1].find('^[A-z0-9]*')
    firstAreaF = firstArea1[1].split('^[A-z0-9]*/')[1]
    
    //get list of firstarea = arealist
    def areaList = firstBody."${firstAfterRoot}".collect { getNodeByPath(it,firstAreaF).text() }
    areaList = areaList.collect{ it.toString().toLowerCase() }
    
    
    secArea1 = secArea.split('^[A-z0-9]*/')
    secAfterRoot = secArea1[1].find('^[A-z0-9]*')
    secAreaF = secArea1[1].split('^[A-z0-9]*/')[1]
    
    def secAreaList = secBody."${secAfterRoot}".collect { getNodeByPath(it,secAreaF).text() }
    secAreaList = secAreaList.collect{ it.toString().toLowerCase() }
    
    //get list of numbers of area in the sec body = secareanum
    def secareanum = []
    for(int i = 0; i < areaList.size(); i++){
        secAreaList.eachWithIndex{it,index->
            if(it.equals(areaList[i])){
                secareanum[i] = index
            }
        }
    }
    

    //xml according to structs 

    def firstStructf = firstStruct.split('^[A-z0-9]*/')
    def secStructf = secStruct.split('^[A-z0-9]*/')
    def firstNode = getNodeByPath(firstBody, firstStructf[1])
    def secNode = getNodeByPath(secBody, secStructf[1])
    def tmpNode
    def inode
    
    def varStringWriter = new StringWriter();
    def indentPrinter = new IndentPrinter(varStringWriter, '    ')
    def varXMLBuilder   = new MarkupBuilder(indentPrinter);
   
    varXMLBuilder.Root {
        for(int i = 0; i < firstNode.size(); i++){
            'Entry' {
                'id' areaList[i]

            }
        }
    }

    def newnode = new XmlParser().parseText(varStringWriter.toString())

    newnode.eachWithIndex{it,index->
        if(firstNode[index]==null){
            tmpNode = null
        }else{
            tmpNode = firstNode[index].children()
        }
        if(secareanum[index]==null){
            tmpNode2 = null
        }else{
            tmpNode2 = secNode[secareanum[index]].children()
        }
        it.appendNode(
            firstNode[0].name(), tmpNode
        )
        it.appendNode(
            secNode[0], tmpNode2
        )
    } 
    message.body = XmlUtil.serialize(newnode)
   
    return message;
}

 

  • The first function getNodeByPath is used for getting the desired node that is given in the path.
  • In the start of the main function, we are getting the properties to variables and parsing the XML’s.
  • We create firstAfterRoot and firstAreaF variables to search the emails in the XML and put them into a list (areaList) then we make it lower case.
  • We do the same thing for the second XML.
  • We create a for loop to create a list (secareanum) for the places of emails in the second XML.

For example,

areaList = [1@email.com, 2@email.com, 3@email.com, , 4@email.com]

secAreaList = [5@email.com, 3@email.com, 2@email.com, 4@email.com, 1@email.com]

secareanum = [4, 2, 1, null,3]

  • We get the structures that are needed (firstNode, secNode).
  • We define variables to build our new XML.
  • Initialize the XML with matching element as id with for loop.
  • Parse the new XML into newnode because we are going to append nodes.
  • Using eachWithIndex to loop with nodes we are appending nodes and controlling the null values with temporary nodes.
  • Lastly, we serialize the newnode and put it as message to return.

In our example first body is like this:

<root>
	<value>
		<mail>1@Email.com</mail>
	</value>
	<value>
		<mail>2@email.com</mail>
	</value>
	<value>
		<mail>3@email.com</mail>
	</value>
	<value>
		<mail></mail>
	</value>
	<value>
		<mail>4@email.com</mail>
	</value>
</root>

Second structure:

<root>
	<Resources>
		<name>firstname5</name>
		<emails>
			<value>5@email.com</value>
		</emails>
	</Resources>
	<Resources>
		<emails>
			<value>3@email.com</value>
		</emails>
	</Resources>
	<Resources>
		<name>firstname2</name>
		<emails>
			<value>2@email.com</value>
		</emails>
	</Resources>
	<Resources>
		<emails>
			<value>4@email.com</value>
		</emails>
	</Resources>
	<Resources>
		<name>firstname</name>
		<emails>
			<value>1@email.com</value>
		</emails>
	</Resources>
</root>

Result’s structure is like this:

 

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<Root>
	<Entry>
		<id>1@email.com</id>
		<value>
			<mail>1@Email.com</mail>
		</value>
		<Resources>
			<name>firstname</name>
			<emails>
				<value>1@email.com</value>
			</emails>
		</Resources>
	</Entry>
	<Entry>
		<id>2@email.com</id>
		<value>
			<mail>2@email.com</mail>
		</value>
		<Resources>
			<name>firstname2</name>
			<emails>
				<value>2@email.com</value>
			</emails>
		</Resources>
	</Entry>
	<Entry>
		<id>3@email.com</id>
		<value>
			<mail>3@email.com</mail>
		</value>
		<Resources>
			<emails>
				<value>3@email.com</value>
			</emails>
		</Resources>
	</Entry>
	<Entry>
		<id/>
		<value>
			<mail/>
		</value>
		<Resources/>
	</Entry>
	<Entry>
		<id>4@email.com</id>
		<value>
			<mail>4@email.com</mail>
		</value>
		<Resources>
			<emails>
				<value>4@email.com</value>
			</emails>
		</Resources>
	</Entry>
</Root>

 

Conclusion:

By integrating Groovy scripts into SAP CPI, you can seamlessly merge XML documents, without using complex mappings or XSLT codes on the flow.

I hope that this blog will help you to merge your documents!

Thank you so much for reading my first blog!

entry.title: Re: Alternative-Based Confirmation - Substitute with strategy
entry.content:
Hi @Andreas_Krause 

Thanks for the detail blog. 

We have a business requirement where we are implementing ABC- Plant Substitution in aATP. The requirement is as follows.

The system should only substitute an alternate plant when originally requested plant has less than 30% stock available. If an alternate is considered, it should provide full confirmation from its stock. If even alternate plant cannot provide full confirmation, then the originally requested plant should be confirmed instead.

Using Force Inline substitution. (No subitem generation).

And we have to consider RLT (Check Horizon = Full confirmation).

Issue Faced:

I was able to achieve this requirement without RLT (Check Horizon = Ignore check Horizon) by configuring strategies as below:

Strategy 1: Attributes: Max confirmation Ratio. Hard Constraint: Confirmation ratio >= 30%, Original plant must be used.

Strategy 2: Attributes: Max confirmation Ratio, Min. Sequence number, Min. Delay. Hard Constraint: Confirmation ratio >= 100%

Strategy 3: Attributes: Max confirmation Ratio, Hard Constraint: Original plant must be used.

However, when RLT is active, the system considers the total available quantity including RLT stock for the original plant. Because of this, ABC does not trigger an alternate plant substitution, even when the available stock (excluding replenishment stock) is below 30%. Adding Min delay is also not working as it substituting with an alternate plant when stock is available in original plant.

Expectation is system should only check actual available stock when deciding whether to switch to alternate plant. It should not include stock from RLT when evaluating 30% threshold for substitution.

Is there any standard configuration or setting available in aATP where we can ensure ABC only checks confirmed quantity from available stock and does not consider RLT quantity when determining plant substitution? Or how strategy can be modified to fulfill requirement?

Any insights or recommendations would be greatly appreciated.
